race.name: Cadel Evans Great Ocean Road Ride
race.display_name: Cadel Evans Great Ocean Road Ride
race.tagline: Australia's Tour de France winner. The Great Ocean Road. Challambra Crescent at 10%. Ride the same roads as the pros.

vitals.registration: Online via cadelevansgreatoceenroadride.com.au. Opens mid-year prior. Cost: AUD $120-180 (~$80-120 USD).
vitals.feed_zones: Multiple feed stations along the route, including coastal and hinterland locations.
vitals.cutoff_time: Course support has time limits. Reasonable pace required.
route_options: Ride the Road: 153 km (95 mi), 1,600m climbing — the full distance sharing pro race roads
Medio: 100 km (62 mi) — shorter coastal option

climate.description: Late January is peak Australian summer. Expect hot conditions — 28-35C (82-95F) is typical, with occasional 40C+ heat waves possible. The coastal location provides some relief via ocean breezes, but inland hinterland sections lose that advantage. UV radiation is extreme. Early morning start helps, but riders finishing in the afternoon face the full force of the Australian sun.
challenges: Peak summer heat: 30-35C is typical, 40C+ possible during heat waves
Extreme UV radiation — Australian sun requires serious sun protection
Coastal headwinds can be strong and energy-sapping on exposed ocean-front sections
Minimal shade on many road sections, especially along the Great Ocean Road

terrain.surface: Well-maintained sealed roads throughout. Mix of coastal highway (Great Ocean Road sections) and Surf Coast hinterland back roads. All tarmac, generally good condition.
features: Challambra Crescent — 1.5 km at 10%+, the decisive climb of the pro race
Great Ocean Road coastal sections with ocean views
Rolling Surf Coast hinterland with short punchy climbs
Exposed coastal sections where crosswinds and headwinds factor heavily

fondo_rating.scoring_notes: Migrated from gravel-race-automation. Original score: 63. adventure dimension dropped, road_surface added. Score recalculated: 63.

course_description.character: A coastal sportive with a sharp sting. The Great Ocean Road sections provide the visual spectacle — crashing surf, dramatic coastal cliffs, wide Pacific views — while the Surf Coast hinterland delivers the physical challenge through relentless short rollers. The route mimics the professional race course, with Challambra Crescent as the decisive climb. It's not a mountain sportive, but the accumulated climbing, the heat, and the coastal wind make 153 km harder than the distance suggests.
course_description.signature_challenge: Challambra Crescent. At 1.5 km, it's over before it starts — but 10%+ gradients on tired legs, in the heat, on the same tarmac that WorldTour professionals contest, make it the most meaningful 1.5 km of the day.

logistics.airport: Melbourne Tullamarine (MEL) — 1.5 hours to Geelong. Melbourne Avalon (AVV) — 30 minutes to Geelong (limited flights). Direct train from Melbourne Southern Cross to Geelong is also available.
logistics.lodging_strategy: Stay in Geelong for proximity to the start/finish and the pro race atmosphere. Hotels run AUD $120-250/night on event weekend. Torquay and Anglesea along the Surf Coast are alternatives with a beachside vibe. Book 2-3 months ahead — the WorldTour race weekend attracts both cyclists and general sports tourists.
logistics.food: Geelong's waterfront dining precinct is excellent. The Surf Coast has strong cafe culture — Torquay in particular is well-served. Carb loading is easy. Feed stations provide standard cycling nutrition.

history.origin_story: Created in 2015 to honor Cadel Evans, who retired from professional cycling in 2015 after a career that included winning the 2011 Tour de France — Australia's only winner of cycling's greatest race. Evans grew up in the Geelong-Barwon Heads area, and the event routes through his home roads along the Great Ocean Road and Surf Coast hinterland. The sportive is paired with a UCI WorldTour professional race, creating a cycling festival weekend.
notable_moments: 2015: Inaugural edition — honoring Cadel Evans' retirement from professional cycling
WorldTour pro race and public sportive create Australia's biggest cycling weekend
Challambra Crescent becomes the signature climb — decisive in both the pro race and the sportive
Event grows to 4,000-5,000 riders across distances
history.reputation: Australia's cycling celebration. Equal parts sportive, festival, and tribute to the country's greatest road cyclist.

biased_opinion.summary: The Cadel Evans Great Ocean Road Ride trades on two things: a legendary name and a legendary road. It delivers on both. Riding the same course that WorldTour professionals race the same weekend — attacking Challambra Crescent where races are decided — gives this sportive a connection to elite cycling that most events can only dream of. The Great Ocean Road provides the visual payoff, and the Geelong-Surf Coast region's cycling culture creates a genuine festival atmosphere. The ride itself is moderate by gran fondo standards — 153 km with 1,600m of climbing won't break experienced riders — but the experience transcends the physical challenge.
strengths: Pro race connection: ride the same roads as WorldTour professionals the same weekend
Challambra Crescent: attack the climb where professional races are decided
Great Ocean Road scenery: one of Australia's most iconic coastal roads
Excellent value and accessibility from Melbourne
weaknesses: Moderate climbing: 1,600m won't satisfy riders seeking a mountain challenge
Australian summer heat: 30-35C+ can make the ride a survival exercise in bad years
Relatively young event (est. 2015) without the historical depth of European sportives
biased_opinion.bottom_line: The closest most of us will get to riding a WorldTour race. The Great Ocean Road and Cadel's name make it worth the trip.

final_verdict.one_liner: Cadel Evans' legacy. The Great Ocean Road. Challambra Crescent at 10%. Ride the pro course the same weekend.
final_verdict.should_you_race: Yes if riding the same roads as WorldTour professionals appeals to you. Yes if the Great Ocean Road and the Cadel Evans connection are meaningful to your cycling fandom. Skip if you need a mountain challenge — this is a coastal sportive with moderate climbing. Skip if Australian summer heat is a dealbreaker.
final_verdict.alternatives: If you want more climbing in Australia: try Peaks Challenge Falls Creek. If you want a coastal sportive in a different hemisphere: try the Mallorca 312. If you want the full WorldTour connection: watch the pro race and ride the sportive back-to-back.

videos.title: Great Ocean Road - A route cyclist should dream about!
videos.description:
The Great Ocean Road is meant to be one of the most scenic coastal roads in the world, with amazing landscapes and features to surprise any visitor. I would like to confirm this, because it was a fantastic adventure cycling along the route, from Warrnambool to Geelong. The attraction which comes to everyone mind is the Twelve Apostles, which I came across just after the town of Port Campbell. The Twelve Apostles, or what remains are huge piles of sandstone, stand just of the coast. And what a sight they are. Just fantastic and beautiful and well worth the the ride to see them. That not mentioning the other landforms: Bay of Island, Bay of Martyrs, London Bridge, Grotto, Arch and Loch Ard Gorge. Then you got the road its self. From flat straight section, to winding section, to long hill climbs and fantastic downhills. It's a ride with a amazing route, with great views and the chance to see a Koala! 

How Crawl Pedal Splash achieved the Great Ocean Road was we got a train from Southern Cross to Warrnambool, costing about $35 to buy the ticket. If your worried about taking the bike on the train, then not to worry. The bikes are stored in the luggage compartment with the guards at the back of the train. Only issue is with  big groups of people my struggle, as I sure they can only take a max of 5 bikes if that. 
The benefits of starting in Warrnambool, is that your travel back to Melbourne with the prevailing winds and you have no deadline to get to Geelong, as there is multiple trains using Myki cards leaving everyday. 
The first day ride from Warrnambool to Port Campbell is about 48miles (77km) and has about 1400ft (426m) for climbing, so a nice easy day of cycling. The highlight of the day are the Bay of Islands, Bay of Martyrs, Grotto, London Bridge and the Arch. All worth stopping to have a look. Stoping at Peterborough for lunch and staying at Port Campbell for the evening. I would recommend the Port Campbell Hostel for cheap accommodation. 
The second day starts off with you seeing the Loch Ard Gorge, followed by the Epic Twelve Apostles and by getting there before 11am, you miss the tour groups. The second day is about 66miles (106km) long and involves in the main, two large climbs totalling 6300ft (1920m). Stopping at the top of the first and largest at Laver Hill for lunch, before descending down to tackle the next hill, with a average section of 11% for about 3km on it. But watch out for the Koalas in the trees, on the second climb. Finally finishing the day at Apollo Bay, where we stayed at the YHA hostel for the evening. 
The next and final day was 76miles (122km) long and was a section of road cyclists should dream about. Beautiful climbs and decents along the coast. It was an epic day of cycling! No tourists attractions to see, just a great ride to enjoy. Stopping at Lorne for lunch and Torquay for coffee, finishing at the end of the Great Ocean Road in Geelong after a days elevation gain of over 7700ft (2346m) Then jumping on the train back to Southern Cross. 

The Stats for the ride are:
Days 3
Nights 2
Length 192miles (309km)
Elevation gain 15603ft (4755m)

videos.title: #ChallengeChallambra
videos.channel: Cadel Evans Great Ocean Road Race
videos.description:
Cadel Evans tackles the Challambra Climb from Cadel Evans Great Ocean Road Race. 

Will you #ChallengeChallambra? Register on Strava now and look out for the new #ChallengeChallambra leaderboard coming to the event website. Start training, take on the 830 metre segment and compare your time with Cadel Evans!

videos.curation_reason: Recent first-person ride-along of the People's Ride with course conditions and specific terrain details including the Challambra climb.

quotes.text: We're heading into the bottom of Challambra - looks a little bit steep at the bottom than I think it might have on TV but quite steep at the top.
It's 22 degrees on the way down to the coast but 32 degrees on the way back - rolling sealed roads through tree-lined sections, basically all downhill for the last half hour down to Torquay.

key_challenges.name: Challambra climb
key_challenges.description: Looks a little bit steep at the bottom than expected from watching on TV, gets quite steep at the top

terrain_notes.text: Roads are good conditions on sealed roads, basically all downhill for about half an hour heading down to Torquay, rolling sealed roads throughout most of the course
Has some gravel at the finish for gravel enthusiasts

race_day_tips.text: Temperature can vary significantly during the ride - was 26 degrees going down to the coast but hit 32 degrees on the way back, despite forecast of 34 degrees
Expect a general regrouping coming into Torquay after the downhill section

additional_quotes.text: Nice roads dead roads but nice roads basically all downhill for the last half an hour or so
Temperature can vary significantly - was 26 degrees going down to the coast but hit 32 degrees on the way back

rider_intel.search_text: The Cadel Evans Great Ocean Road Race features mostly sealed roads in good condition with a significant downhill section lasting about 30 minutes heading down to Torquay, followed by rolling terrain. The route goes through Moriak down to Bells Beach and includes tree-lined roads. A key challenge is the Challambra climb near the end, which appears steeper than it looks on TV, particularly at the bottom and top sections. Temperature conditions can vary dramatically during the ride, with one rider experiencing 26 degrees on the coastal descent but 32 degrees on the return journey. The course includes some gravel sections at the finish line. Riders report general regrouping opportunities coming into Torquay after the downhill sections. The route passes notable landmarks including 13th Beach and finishes with a roll back into Geelong around the botanic gardens.
